With the increasing application of complex physical systems, sys-
temmodeling has become a crucial task in engineering and scientific
research. Modelica, an object-oriented, multidisciplinary modeling
language [24], is widely used across various fields [25] such as
mechanical [16], electrical[17] [19], electronic[18], hydraulic[20],
thermal[21], and control engineering [22] [23]. It represents system
behavior through standardized mathematical equations, offering
engineers a convenient and efficient tool for the secondary devel-
opment of simulation models.
∗*Corresponding authors

Model-Based Workflow for
a Code Generation

To illustrate the capabilities of Modelica in simulating dynamic
physical systems, we present a example in Listing 1: a simple model
of a bouncing ball. This model demonstrates how the behavior of a
physical object under the influence of gravity can be represented
in Modelica. The ball’s motion is described by a set of ordinary dif-
ferential equations, where its height and velocity are updated over
time based on gravitational acceleration. Additionally, the model
incorporates the coefficient of restitution to simulate the elasticity
of the ball during collisions with the ground. The simulation re-
sults, showing the variation in height and velocity of the bouncing
ball, are depicted in Figure 1. This example effectively showcases
Modelica’s ability to model and simulate complex physical systems.
1  model  BouncingBall
2  constant  Real g=9.81 "Gravity  constant";
3  parameter  Real c=0.9 "Coefficient  of  restitution";
4  parameter  Real  radius=0.1 "Radius  of the  ball";
5  Real  height(start=1) "Height  of the  ball  center";
6  Real  velocity(start=0) "Velocity  of the  ball";
7  equation
8  der(height) = velocity;
9  der(velocity) = -g;
10  when  height <= redius  then
11  reinit(velocity ,-c*pre(velocity ));
12  end  when;
13  end  BouncingBall;
Listing 1: A Simple Model of a Bouncing Ball.
Figure 1: The Simulation Curve of Height and Velocity Vari-
ation of Bouncing Ball.
Despite its versatility and widespread adoption, effectively lever-
aging Modelica for complex system modeling is challenging. Cre-
ating and optimizing Modelica models often demands extensive
domain expertise and considerable time, leading to a cumbersome
and error-prone process. In recent years, the rapid development of
large language models (LLMs) in code generation and software en-
gineering has opened up new possibilities for automating the Mod-
elica modeling process. Currently, many LLMs have demonstrated

In this section, we first outline the two primary research tasks
aimed at enhancing the modeling process with LLMs. Then, we
introduce the benchmark datasets used for performance evaluation,
which correspond to these two research tasks.
2.1  Research Tasks
This study systematically evaluates the generative capabilities of
LLMs for Modelica modeling by addressing two research tasks:
component generation and test case generation. Leveraging
LLMs generation to Modelica code generation not only improves
modeling efficiency and reliability but also accelerates the develop-
ment of intelligent modeling tools. A detailed description of each
task is provided as follows, outlining the objectives and design
methodology of them.
Task 1: Component Generation. The first task focuses on
generating Modelica components that meet specific design require-
ments, thereby improving the efficiency and accuracy of system
modeling. Accurate components are fundamental to constructing
complex system models in Modelica. However, their manual cre-
ation is both time-consuming and requires specialized expertise.
This task explores the potential of LLMs to automate the generation
of such components, addressing challenges such as low modeling
efficiency and the dispersion of learning resources.
Task 2: Test Case Generation. The second task aims to auto-
mate the generation of test cases for existing Modelica components,
enhancing testing efficiency and reliability. As the Modelica library
continues to expand, model testing has become increasingly critical
for ensuring the correct behavior and functionality of individual
components within larger systems. Effective testing helps detect
errors early in the development process, thereby improving overall
model quality and reliability. This research task focuses on generat-
ing test cases that encompass a diverse range of scenarios, aiming
to establish a more efficient and robust testing framework for sup-
porting engineering design and system simulation.
2.2  Benchmark Datasets
However, there is no universally accepted benchmark or standard-
ized dataset for evaluating Modelica code generation. Fortunately,
the Modelica ecosystem offers extensive resources to support model

In this section, we evaluate the performance of LLMs on the afore-
mentioned tasks using benchmark datasets. We first introduce the
metrics used to assess the generated results, followed by an evalua-
tion and analysis of the baseline performance.
3.1  Evaluation Metrics
To evaluate the performance of LLMs in generating Modelica com-
ponents and test cases, we designed a comprehensive evaluation
framework and established suitable metrics. Both the component
dataset and the tested component dataset consist of Modelica code.
The primary objective is to assess whether the generated code
meets the required syntactic and functional correctness.
The Modelica code validation process follows these steps: First, a
widely-usedModelica tool is selected, such as OpenModelica [15] to
support compilation and simulation of the generated code. The code
is then passed through the validation module, which consists of
four key steps: load validation, checking validation, simulation
validation, and functional validation. The first three of these
validation steps are automated using the OMPython interface in
OpenModelica [1].
• Load validation verifies the generated code conforms to
the basic syntax and structure of Modelica.
• Checking validation inspects the code for errors such as in-
correct parameter types, omission of component invocation,
or violations of Modelica’s structural constraints.
• Simulation validation ensures that the code can execute
simulations successfully, without issues such as uninitialized
1Instruction Templates.
2Exemplary Test Case Prompt.

To enhance the performance of LLMs in generating Modelica code,
this study introduces a general workflow that integrates three strate-
gic approaches. These strategies are designed to improve syntactic
accuracy, structural integrity, and physical function consistency,
ensuring that the generated code complies with the specific require-
ments of the Modelica language. By targeting various aspects of
the generation process, the strategies not only improve the overall
quality of the generated code but also enhance the LLM’s adapt-
ability and robustness across diverse tasks. The proposed workflow
provides a systematic framework to effectively apply the enhance-
ment strategies. The detailedworkflow and individual enhancement
strategies are described in the following sections.

ModiGen: A Large Language Model-Based Workflow for Multi-Task Modelica Code Gen

flow Architecture.
4.1 Workflow
Overall, we proposeModiGen, an innovative workflow for Modelica
test case and component generation, designed to automate and facil-
itate complexmodeling tasks across various domains. The workflow
architecture illustrated in Figure 2 consists of three main stages: (1)
data preprocessing and fine-tuning, (2) code generation enhanced
by GraphRAG, and (3) validation with feedback optimization. In the
first stage, ModiGen preprocesses collected Modelica code into a
standardized format, preparing it for subsequent fine-tuning. In the
second stage, the fine-tuned LLM collaborates with the GraphRAG
module to generate Modelica code based on provided prompts. Fi-
nally, in the third stage, the generated code undergoes rigorous
simulation and validation, which includes syntax and functionality
validation. If validation fails, iterative optimization based on feed-
back is performed to refine the code. Once successfully validated,
the code can be utilized for functional testing or further modeling
tasks. To facilitate further research, we have made our source code
3
publicly available.
4.2  Fine-tuning
To enhance the performance of LLMs in generating Modelica code,
ModiGen adopts a targeted fine-tuning approach to better adapt the
models to the syntactic and structural characteristics of Modelica.
Data Preparation: To effectively leverage LLMs for advancing
Modelica applications, it is essential to prepare a substantial corpus
of Modelica code datasets for fine-tuning. Despite the extensive
libraries accumulated by the Modelica community and commer-
cial organizations, these repositories remain decentralized, lacking
systematic organization and structured data extraction. Thus, a
standardized approach to data annotation and conversion is neces-
sary to ensure systematic organization and extraction of Modelica
code datasets, improving their quality, accessibility, and usability.
To bridge this gap, we collected data from 44 open-source and
commercial Modelica libraries, each containing a wide range of
component types from diverse scientific and engineering fields.
The core task of the data preprocessing phase is to convert raw
Modelica files into a machine-readable format. We first process the
.mo files of these libraries using the methods described in Section
2.2, including content extraction, data cleaning, and formatting. A

data filtering step is then applied to remove samples that do not
meet predefined criteria. The cleaning process includes:
• Removing code lacking description and documentation fields
to ensure that each code includes essential functional infor-
mation;
• Filtering out code exceeding a predefined size threshold to
prevent excessive complexity from hindering analysis;
• Excluding non-modeling code that lacks actual component
data, including, but not limited to, "UserGuide" and "Icon"
files, which typically contain usage instructions or graphical
representations rather than modeling information.
After completing these steps, 𝐷𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑒𝑡𝑎𝑙𝑙 was created, ensuring
consistency and structured formatting for subsequent data process-
ing and modeling analysis.
To improve the accuracy of LLMs in Modelica generation tasks,
we performed supervised fine-tuning.

This process is critical since
precise instruction are essential for activating the adaptation of
LLMs to specific domains. The Modelica code, descriptions, param-
eters, and documentation from 𝐷𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑒𝑡𝑎𝑙𝑙 were batch-processed
using the GPT-4-turbo API to generate customized prompts. The
instruction templates in Section2.2 were employed, with modifi-
cations tailored specifically to fine-tuning data and corresponding
keywords.
Based on the code from the six libraries referenced in Section 2.2,
we formatted the generated prompts along with the correspond-
ing code to create the instruction-supervised dataset, 𝐷𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑒𝑡𝑠 𝑓 𝑡 .
The dataset follows this format: "{"instruction": "", "query
(prompt)": "", "response(model)": ""}".
After preprocessing and de-duplication, 𝐷𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑒𝑡𝑎𝑙𝑙 includes 9,456
Modelica instances, while 𝐷𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑒𝑡𝑠 𝑓 𝑡 comprises 3,202 instances.
Figure 3 illustrates the distribution of different structural types in
𝐷𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑒𝑡𝑎𝑙𝑙 and 𝐷𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑒𝑡𝑠 𝑓 𝑡 , providing insights into their structure
and diversity.
LLMs and Method: For fine-tuning, We select the represen-
tative open-source LLMs as in baseline evaluation. These LLMs,
with their extensive pre-training knowledge and robust code gen-
eration capabilities, provide a solid foundation for Modelica code
generation.
To efficiently adapt these LLMs to the Modelica domain, we em-
ploy the Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) technique, LoRA
[54], which reduces computational overhead and eliminates the

Conference acronym ’XX, June 03–05, 2018, Woodstock, NY
need for large-scale retraining by applying low-rank decompo-
sition to LLMs parameters. This method significantly enhances
fine-tuning efficiency.
During fine-tuning, we adjust several critical hyperparameters to
optimize performance within the Modelica domain. Specifically, the
initial learning rate is set at 5e-4, and the batch size is configured to
1 per device to optimize computational resource management. The
training is restricted to 10 epochs to mitigate the risk of overfitting
while ensuring sufficient exposure to the learning process. Addition-
ally, a cosine learning rate scheduler is employed to dynamically
adjust the learning rate, promoting better LLMs convergence and
potentially enhancing generalization capabilities across various
modeling tasks. These parameters are chosen for their impact on
training efficiency, ensuring optimal fine-tuning and adaptation to
the Modelica domain while balancing resource utilization.
The fine-tuning process consists of two stages. First, unsuper-
vised fine-tuning is performed on 𝐷𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑒𝑡𝑎𝑙𝑙 . This step enables
the LLM to better grasp Modelica’s syntax, structure, and usage
patterns by leveraging rich contextual and domain-specific infor-
mation, thereby improving its generalization ability. Subsequently,
instruction-supervised fine-tuning is conducted using 𝐷𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑒𝑡𝑠 𝑓 𝑡 ,
which consists of structured prompts paired with corresponding
code. This step refines the LLM’s ability to generate Modelica code
under specific task constraints.
Through this fine-tuning strategy, the LLM gains a deeper un-
derstanding of Modelica’s object-oriented and component-based
structure, enabling it to generate high-quality code that adheres to
the domain’s specific requirements.
4.3  GraphRAG
To enhance knowledge-intensive tasks and effectively leverage ex-
ternal knowledge graphs, we employ Graph Retrieval-Augmented
Generation (GraphRAG) in Modelica code generation. This ap-
proach integrates graph-based structures with retrieval-augmented
generation (RAG) methods, enabling generative models to retrieve
and utilize structured information from a graph in real time.
Given the non-causal, equation-based nature of the Modelica
language, along with its object-oriented design and support for
multi-domain modeling, capturing and applying structured domain
knowledge is essential for improving code quality and accuracy.
Modelica encompasses a vast body of knowledge spanning mul-
tiple engineering disciplines. Traditional fine-tuning approaches
often struggle to comprehensively encode this knowledge, par-
ticularly when dealing with domain-specific literature, technical
documentation, and case studies. Effectively incorporating this
external knowledge into the code-generation process presents a sig-
nificant challenge. Inspired by recent advances in integrating graph
structures with LLMs [57, 58], we employ GraphRAG to enhance
structured knowledge retrieval and dependency resolution, ensur-
ing that the generated Modelica code adheres to domain constraints
and accurately reflects cross-domain interactions.
We construct a domain-specific Property Graph tailored to our
task.

We formulated four research questions (RQs) to systematically
evaluate the performance of LLMs in generating Modelica models:
RQ1: To what extent does our proposed workflow improve LLM
generation performance compared to the baseline?

RQ2: How do different enhancement strategies individually im-
pact the quality of generated Modelica code?
The performance evaluation of existing LLMs on Modelica com-
ponent and test case generation tasks has been comprehensively
covered in the baseline analysis, detailed in Section 3.2. In this
section, we focus primarily on answering RQ1 and RQ2 through
comprehensive empirical analyses, providing further insights into
the effectiveness of our proposed approaches and strategies.
5.1  Evaluation of Workflow
In response to RQ1, we conducted a series of experiments to sys-
tematically evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed workflow for
Modelica code generation, focusing on component and test case
generation tasks. We quantified the overall performance improve-
ments by comparing the application of the workflow to the baseline,
specifically measuring the 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑓 @𝑘 metrics to demonstrate how
the complete workflow enhances Modelica code generations.
Table 2 presents the enhanced performance of LLMs on the
two Modelica generation tasks using our proposed workflow, re-
porting 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑓 @1 and 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑓 @𝑠𝑐𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑟𝑖𝑜 metrics along with their
improvements over the baseline. Notably, the proprietary LLMs
skipped the fine-tuning module, relying solely on the other en-
hancement strategies, as their closed-source nature prevents direct
access to LLMs weights for fine-tuning. Among the evaluated mod-
els, CodeLlama-34B demonstrates the best performance in both
metrics for the component generation task, outperforming even
the proprietary models GPT-4o and Claude-3 in this specific task.
For the test case generation task, StarCoder2-15B achieves the high-
est 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑓 @𝑠𝑐𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑟𝑖𝑜 score, while Claude-3 outperforms all other
models in 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑓 @1. Regarding Δ𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑓 @𝑘, in the component gen-
eration task, CodeLlama-7B exhibits the greatest improvement in
Δ𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑓 @1, whereas CodeLlama-34B achieves the highest gain in
Δ𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑓 @𝑠𝑐𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑟𝑖𝑜. For the test case generation task, StarCoder2-
15B exhibits the most significant improvement in both metrics.
5.2  Evaluation of Enhancement Strategies
To address RQ2, we further analyze the effectiveness of our pro-
posed enhancement strategies. We evaluate the individual and com-
bined contributions of supervised fine-tuning, GraphRAG, and feed-
back optimization. Figure 6 provides a visual representation of these
performance improvements, illustrating the relative impact of each
strategy and their combined effect on Modelica code generation.
The following sections present a detailed analysis, examining their
respective contributions to overall performance.
5.2.1  Fine-tuning. We fine-tuned the selected open-source LLMs
on domain-specific data (denoted as SFT in Figure 6) to enhance

their adaptability to the Modelica code generation task. The results
in Figure 6 show that fine-tuning improves performance across
both 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠@1 and 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠@𝑠𝑐𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑟𝑖𝑜 metrics.
In the component generation task, all models after fine-tuning
exhibited significant improvements in 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠@1 and 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠@𝑠𝑐𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑟𝑖𝑜.
This indicates that fine-tuning effectively expands the coverage of
generated outputs while substantially enhancing the models’ ability
to handle complex scenarios.
In the test case generation task, fine-tuning also resulted in
notable improvements across both metrics, though the gains were
more moderate compared to component generation. This trend
aligns with the performance observed in the component generation
task, suggesting that domain-specific fine-tuning remains stable
and generalizable across different Modelica modeling tasks.
Furthermore, while larger models generally achieve better per-
formance, the benefits of fine-tuning do not scale proportionally
with the number of parameters.

This may be due to limitations in
the benchmark dataset, which might not fully leverage the potential
of larger models in terms of coverage and diversity.
5.2.2  GraphRAG. We exclusively apply the GraphRAGmechanism
(denoted as GRAG in Figure 6) to the component generation task
due to its adeptness at handling complex dependencies. GraphRAG
significantly enhances the LLM’s ability to discern structural rela-
tionships within component generation tasks, which often involve
intricate cross-module dependencies. It achieves this by leverag-
ing comprehensive retrieval information and semantic associations
to improve understanding and execution. In test case generation,

Strategies in Modelica Code Generation
where dependencies are relatively simple, a hard-coded approach
is preferred. Specifically, component dependency information (the
structural details that the component simulation needs to "extend")
is directly incorporated into the prompt and provided to the LLMs
to achieve better results.
The results presented in Figure 6 demonstrate substantial im-
provements in both 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠@1 and 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠@𝑠𝑐𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑟𝑖𝑜 metrics after ap-
plying GraphRAG. Additionally, a comparison of the improvements
in 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑠@1 and 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑓 @1 in Table 3 reveals that, for most LLMs, the
performance gains in 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑠@1 achieved through the GraphRAG
mechanism are lower than those in 𝑝𝑎𝑠𝑠𝑓 @1. This suggests that
GraphRAG has a more pronounced effect on enhancing physical
consistency and adherence to modeling principles in Modelica com-
ponent generation. This is likely because the retrieval-enhanced
information primarily consists of technical documents containing
modeling principles and physical constraints.
Additional experiments indicate that the performance of Graph-
RAG is significantly influenced by the quality of the structured
information provided by the retrieval module. Notably, outcomes
utilizing the attribute graph index consistently demonstrate supe-
rior performance compared to those using a general vector index.
This observation highlights the pivotal role of GraphRAG in in-
tegrating graph-based features into the generation of modeling
languages.
5.2.3  Feedback Optimization. For the generated Modelica mod-
els, we employ a feedback optimization strategy (denoted as FB
in Figure 6) to progressively enhance output quality. In theory,.

Despite the substantial performance improvements achieved through
the current workflow, limitations persist in Modelica code gener-
ation. This section discusses the limitations based on evaluation
results and suggests potential directions for future research.

The rapid advancements in large language models (LLMs) have
significantly influenced the field of automated code generation.

Trovato et al.
Meanwhile, system modeling has become increasingly critical for
designing and analyzing complex engineering systems across vari-
ous domains. This section reviews key developments in three as-
pects: general code generation, model-based generation, and the
application of Modelica in system modeling.
1. Code Generation
Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have signifi-
cantly accelerated research in code engineering [5, 6], particularly
in automated code generation. The Transformer architecture [37]
laid the foundation for modern LLMs with its self-attention mech-
anism. Codex [32], an extension of GPT-3 [39], powers GitHub
Copilot, significantly enhancing code-writing efficiency. Beyond
Codex, large-scale models such as CodeGen [41], AlphaCode [42],
Claude 3 [43], Gemini [44], CodeLlama [33], Qwen2.5-Coder [2],
and DeepSeek-Coder [45] leverage billions of parameters and ex-
tensive high-quality code corpora to push the boundaries of code
generation. These advancements continue to refine the accuracy,
generalization, and efficiency of automated programming.
2. Modelica and Model-Based Generation
Modelica, an equation-based, object-oriented, and non-causal
modeling language, has emerged as a powerful tool for simulating
multi-domain systems. Its capability to support continuous-discrete
simulations and integrate multiple physical domains has facilitated
its widespread adoption across diverse fields, including electric
vehicles [48], hydraulic systems [49], thermodynamic systems [50],
electric power systems [51], and chemical processes [52]. These
applications highlightModelica’s effectiveness inmodeling complex
physical systems (CPS), establishing it as a fundamental tool for
advancing system-level simulation and analysis.
Despite its versatility, generating Modelica code remains a chal-
lenging task, particularly for domain experts who may not be pro-
ficient in programming. This challenge aligns with broader difficul-
ties in code generation for Low-Resource Programming Languages
(LRPLs) and Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs), where limited
datasets hinder the training of LLMs [46]. Addressing this limita-
tion requires leveraging alternative data sources or employing data
synthesis techniques. Gu et al. [47] investigated domain-specific
code generation and found that LLMs often struggle with special-
ized libraries due to their limited exposure during pretraining. To
mitigate this issue, they introduced DomCoder, a novel approach
designed to enhance domain-specific code generation.
Beyond domain-specific languages, LLMs have also been ex-
plored for software modeling tasks. Wang et al. [36] empirically
demonstrated the potential of LLMs to generate Unified Modeling
Language (UML) artifacts, such as use case models, class diagrams,
and sequence diagrams, while also identifying key limitations in
this process. Similarly, Cámara et al. [35] analyzed the performance
of LLMs, such as ChatGPT, in software modeling and highlighted
challenges, including syntactic and semantic inaccuracies, response
inconsistency, and scalability constraints. These studies suggest
that while LLMs show promise in model-based generation, their
effectiveness in generating Modelica code remains an open research
question.
To address this gap, our work systematically investigates the
capability of LLMs in generating Modelica code. To facilitate a com-
prehensive evaluation, we establish a set of benchmarks and apply
a customized workflow tailored for domain-specific, physics-based

This study investigates the performance of large language models
(LLMs) in generating Modelica code. To advance this research, we
developed a benchmark suite for Modelica code generation that
standardizes LLM evaluation across two distinct tasks and enables
comprehensive performance analysis. To improve generation qual-
ity, we propose an effective enhancement workflow that integrates
fine-tuning, GraphRAG, and feedback optimization. A diverse se-
lection of open-source and proprietary models was evaluated to
assess their performance. The results indicate that while LLMs can
generate Modelica code with reasonable accuracy, the proposed
enhancement workflow significantly improves performance. Over-
all, this study highlights the potential of LLMs in Modelica code
generation and provides valuable insights into their capabilities
and limitations in system modeling. Further research in this area
could lead to the development of more advanced and flexible tools
for modeling and engineering applications.
